---
pagetitle: "Autonomous Navigation for the Polaris GEM e2"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero-section}

# Autonomous Navigation for the Polaris GEM e2 {.title}

::: {.subtitle}
Precision Lane Tracking and Intelligent Parking for a Modern Robotaxi.
:::

::: {.author-list}
[**Aditya Korlahalli**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/adityakorlahalli/)^1^,
[**Gokul Narayan**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/-gokulnarayan/)^1^,
[**Ishaan Kandamuri**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ishaan-kandamuri-8a02141b2/)^1^,
[**Shyam Peden**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/shyam-peden-89a715267/)^1^
:::

::: {.affiliation-list}
^1^University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
:::


:::

::: {.section-container}
::: {.hero-teaser}
![GEM car that we will be programming](static/images/gem_real.png){.teaser-img}
:::
:::

::: {.section-container}
## Abstract {.section-title}

::: {.abstract-text}
We will be working on a full-scale autonomous vehicle, the Polaris GEM e2. Using this car and what we have learned throughout the semester, we plan on implementing several features. The first feature is the most obvious: have the car stay in the lanes. Whether the car is on a straight or a turn, the car must stay in the lane to ensure proper functionality. We also want our car to be able to detect obstacles on the road. This all relates to us wanting to generate an autonomous car that potentially could drive on a real road. If the car detects a stop sign, we want our car to stop for a certain amount of time to simulate what a car should do in the real world. Similarly, we want the car to stop in an emergency if an obstacle is detected in the car’s path. 
:::
:::

<!-- ============================================================ -->

<!-- BLOCK DIAGRAM -->

<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}
## Block Diagram {.section-title}

::: {.result-card}
![Block Diagram for Implementation on GEM e2](static/images/block_diagram.png){.block_diagram-img}
:::

::: {.content-text}
Above is our block diagram that we plan on implementing. Using the cameras and sensors on the car, we will get our needed information to pass into the computer. Once we have this, our program will run considering the values and send information to help with the car's decision-making process. For example, we want to follow inside the lanes. So, we want to make a decision to speed or slow down, and turn the steering wheel a certain way based on where in the lane we are. Finally, we will adjust the speed of the car, turn the wheel if needed, and update the current position.
:::
:::

<!-- ============================================================ -->

<!-- OVERVIEW / METHOD VIDEO -->

<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.section-container}
## Simulation Video of Lane Detection, Following, and Pure Pursuit Control {.section-title}

::: {.video-container}
{{< video static/videos/sim_lane_detection.mov>}}
:::

::: {.content-text}
In this video, we are running our lane detection, lane following, and pure pursuit control code in the simulation. 
As you can see, we are able to detect the lanes and automatically steer to stay within the lines. We had to experiment a lot with speed, how much to steer, and how to avoid the car recognizing the cones as lanes.
After much testing, we were able to create a proper lane following algorithm that is ready for the Highbay car. The car finished 3 laps without failure.
:::
:::

::: {.section-container}
## Lane Detection in Real-life Highbay {.section-title}

::: {.video-container}
{{< video static/videos/irl_lane_detection.mov>}}
:::

::: {.content-text}
The car was able to autonomously move given our controller. One issue as you can see in the video was that we did not handle the sharp turns well.
We attribute this to the gap from sim to irl and we know if we had more time we could have fixed this issue.
:::
:::


::: {.section-container}
## Rosbag Replay from IRL Driving {.section-title}

::: {.video-container}
{{< video static/videos/rosbagvideo_front_cam.mov >}}
:::

::: {.content-text}
Here is the IRL driving replay from our Rosbag.
:::
:::

::: {.section-container}
## Obstacle Detection in Simulation {.section-title}

::: {.video-container}
{{< video static/videos/obstacle_avoid.mov>}}
:::

::: {.content-text}
In our simulation, we were able to get the car to detect hazards in the lane and stop immediately. It doesn't resume until they are removed.
It also was able to detect an obstacle that serves as a stop sign, wait for 2 seconds, and continue to drive.
:::
:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->

<!-- Conclusion and Remarks -->

<!-- ============================================================ -->


::: {.section-container}
## Conclusion and Remarks {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}
Overall, we would have been able to fully integrate if we had more time with the hardware after implementing the lane following in simulation.
We definitely could have tuned the steering gains better and made a fully working controller. We found that the gap from simulation to real-life was the biggest hurdle due to our inexperience with dealing with the real-life camera extrinsics.
With this increase in time, we also think we could have easily implemented the car finding the lane immediately from a parking spot and going to a given GPS point.
:::
:::

<!-- ============================================================ -->

<!-- BIBTEX -->

<!-- ============================================================ -->


::: {.section-container}
## BibTeX {.section-title}

```bibtex
@article{AutonomousNavigationPolarisGEMe2,
  author    = {Aditya Korlahalli and Gokul Narayan and Ishaan Kandamuri and Shyam Peden},
  title     = {Autonomous Navigation for the Polaris GEM e2},
  journal   = {ECE 484 Final Project},
  year      = {2026},
}
```

:::

<!-- ============================================================ -->

<!-- FOOTER -->

<!-- ============================================================ -->


::: {.site-footer}



This website template is adapted from the

[Nerfies](https://nerfies.github.io) project page, which is licensed under a

[Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International License](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/).



:::
